snippets.title: Basic heapq usage
snippets.code:
import heapq

pq: list[int] = []
for v in [5, 1, 8, 3, 2]:
    heapq.heappush(pq, v)        # O(log n) each

print(pq[0])                      # 1   - peek (root) is O(1)
print(heapq.heappop(pq))         # 1   - smallest popped
print(heapq.heappop(pq))         # 2
print(pq)                         # remaining heap (NOT fully sorted)
snippets.explanation: heapq is just functions over a plain list; `pq[0]` is the minimum.

snippets.title: heapify is O(n), not O(n log n)
snippets.code:
import heapq

latencies = [120, 30, 80, 5, 200, 45, 65]
heapq.heapify(latencies)         # O(n) — clever bottom-up sift
print(latencies[0])               # 5   - smallest at the root
print(heapq.heappop(latencies))  # 5
snippets.explanation: Building from an existing array via heapify is faster than pushing one by one.

snippets.title: Top-k with a size-k min-heap
snippets.explanation: Use heapreplace (one comparison, one O(log k) sift) instead of push+pop. O(n log k) overall.

snippets.title: Max-heap by negation
snippets.code:
import heapq

scores = [0.42, 0.91, 0.55, 0.88]
max_heap = [(-s, i) for i, s in enumerate(scores)]
heapq.heapify(max_heap)
worst_score, idx = heapq.heappop(max_heap)
print(-worst_score, idx)   # 0.91 1
snippets.explanation: heapq is min-only; flip the sign on push, flip again on pop.

snippets.title: nlargest / nsmallest for one-shot top-k
snippets.code:
import heapq

times = [120, 30, 80, 5, 200, 45, 65]
print(heapq.nlargest(3, times))    # [200, 120, 80]
print(heapq.nsmallest(3, times))   # [5, 30, 45]

# With a key function:
chunks = [{'id': 'A', 'score': 0.42}, {'id': 'B', 'score': 0.91}]
print(heapq.nlargest(1, chunks, key=lambda c: c['score']))
snippets.explanation: For one-shot top-k these helpers are concise and use a size-k heap internally.
